_comment: AWS AIP-C01 EASY question bank. Approachable, exam-style questions (mostly single-answer, shorter scenarios). To add questions, append objects to 'questions'. Each: {id (unique, e.g. '1.4-13'), task (key in 'tasks'), type ('single'=one correct, 'multi'=all-or-nothing), question (string), options:[{text, correct(bool), explanation}]}. 'multi' questions need >=2 correct:true. Optional 'source' field shows a badge in the app. Per-task 'resources' show as 'read more' links in review.
exam: AWS Certified Generative AI Developer – Professional (AIP-C01)

resources.label: Models at a glance (Bedrock FM selection)
Prepare ML data with SageMaker Data Wrangler

questions.question: A team is reviewing a new Amazon Bedrock workload and wants AWS-curated best practices that are specific to generative AI (prompt engineering, RAG accuracy, responsible AI, model selection) rather than generic guidance. Which resource should they use?

options.text: The AWS Well-Architected Generative AI Lens
options.explanation: The Generative AI Lens extends the Well-Architected Framework with design principles and best practices tailored to GenAI workloads.

options.text: The AWS Well-Architected Framework six pillars only
options.explanation: The core pillars are general-purpose; the GenAI-specific guidance lives in the Generative AI Lens.
Trusted Advisor gives cost/security/limits checks, not GenAI architectural best practices.
Config evaluates resource compliance against rules; it is not a source of GenAI design guidance.

questions.question: A company wants to formally review a generative AI workload against AWS best practices, identify high-risk issues (HRIs), and produce an improvement plan. Which tool should they use?

options.text: AWS Well-Architected Tool with the Generative AI Lens applied
options.explanation: The WA Tool runs a structured review against the framework and any selected lens, surfaces HRIs, and generates an improvement plan.
CloudWatch monitors runtime metrics; it does not perform architectural reviews or produce improvement plans.
Cost Explorer analyzes spend, not architectural risk.
Artifact provides compliance reports/agreements, not workload reviews.

questions.question: A generative AI application sometimes receives more requests than the foundation model endpoint can serve, and the team wants the system to degrade gracefully rather than fail outright. Which Well-Architected pillar most directly addresses this design goal?

options.explanation: Graceful degradation, failover, and handling throttling are Reliability-pillar concerns.
Cost Optimization is about spending efficiently, not resilience to load.
Sustainability targets environmental impact, not failure handling.
Operational Excellence covers run/observe/improve processes; resilient behavior under failure maps to Reliability.

questions.question: To control generative AI inference spend, an architect routes simple prompts to a smaller model and reserves a larger model for complex prompts, and uses distillation to create cheaper task-specific models. Which Well-Architected pillar do these decisions primarily serve?

options.explanation: Right-sizing model choice, prompt routing, and distillation to cut inference cost are Cost Optimization practices.
Security covers protecting data and workloads, not spend.
Reliability is about resilience and recovery, not cost.
Performance Efficiency is related (model selection) but the explicit goal here is reducing cost.

questions.question: A team is applying the Reliability pillar to a Bedrock-based application that calls foundation models. Which TWO patterns improve resilience to FM call failures and regional issues?

options.text: Implement circuit breakers in an AWS Step Functions workflow that stop calling the FM when error rates spike
options.explanation: Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures by halting calls to a failing dependency.

options.text: Configure cross-Region failover so requests shift to a healthy Region
options.explanation: Cross-Region failover keeps the workload available during a regional impairment.

options.text: Increase the model temperature to add response variety
options.explanation: Temperature affects output randomness, not reliability.

options.text: Store all prompts in a single Amazon S3 bucket in one Region
options.explanation: A single-Region store is a single point of failure, the opposite of resilient design.
Removing retries reduces, not improves, resilience to transient failures.

questions.question: When an FM call fails, a system returns a cheaper cached or templated response instead of erroring out, preserving a usable experience. What is this pattern called?

options.explanation: Falling back to a reduced-quality but functional response when a dependency fails is graceful degradation.
Provisioned throughput reserves model capacity; it is not a fallback pattern.
Blue/green is a deployment-cutover strategy, unrelated to runtime fallback.
That is a model-training technique, not a resilience pattern.

questions.question: A reviewer wants to ensure a GenAI workload encrypts data, enforces least-privilege access to models, and filters harmful content. Which Well-Architected pillar groups these concerns?

options.explanation: Data protection, least-privilege IAM, and content safeguards (guardrails) are Security-pillar best practices.
Operational Excellence is about running and improving workloads, not data protection controls.
Performance Efficiency targets effective resource use, not protection.
Reliability concerns availability and recovery, not access control and encryption.

questions.question: An ML platform team wants to standardize model versioning and tie model promotion into a CI/CD pipeline so deployments are repeatable and auditable. Which Well-Architected pillar does this best support, and with which service?

options.text: Operational Excellence — using the SageMaker Model Registry in a CI/CD pipeline
options.explanation: Versioned model catalogs feeding CI/CD are an Operational Excellence practice; the Model Registry provides this.
Budgets track spend; they do not provide model versioning or deployment automation.
WAF protects web apps from exploits; it is unrelated to model versioning.
Route 53 is DNS/failover; it does not manage model versions or CI/CD.

questions.question: A team wants to change which foundation model a production application uses without redeploying application code. Which AWS service enables this configuration-driven change, supporting the Operational Excellence pillar?

options.explanation: AppConfig externalizes runtime configuration (such as the model ID) so it can change without a code deployment.
CodeDeploy automates deployments of new code/artifacts, which is exactly what the team wants to avoid here.
Provisioned Throughput reserves model capacity; it does not switch models via config.
Patch Manager handles OS patching, unrelated to model selection.

questions.question: A sustainability-focused review of a GenAI workload asks the team to minimize the environmental impact of inference. Which action best aligns with the Sustainability pillar?

options.text: Choose the smallest model that meets accuracy needs and use managed, efficiently-utilized services
options.explanation: Right-sizing models and maximizing utilization of managed infrastructure reduces energy/resource consumption.

options.text: Always select the largest available model for every task
options.explanation: Oversized models waste compute and energy, contradicting sustainability.

options.text: Run idle GPU endpoints continuously to avoid cold starts
options.explanation: Keeping idle accelerators running wastes resources.

options.text: Duplicate the workload into every Region
options.explanation: Unnecessary global duplication increases resource consumption.

questions.question: During a Well-Architected review, the WA Tool flags several "High Risk Issues" (HRIs) for a GenAI workload. What is the correct interpretation of an HRI?

options.text: An issue likely to have a significant negative impact that should be prioritized for remediation
options.explanation: HRIs are the highest-priority findings the WA Tool surfaces for an improvement plan.

options.text: A billing anomaly detected in Cost Explorer
options.explanation: HRIs come from the architectural review, not billing analysis.

options.text: A model hallucination logged at runtime
options.explanation: HRIs are review findings about design, not runtime model outputs.

options.text: A mandatory AWS service quota increase
options.explanation: HRIs are risk findings, not quota requests.

questions.question: An architect wants to reduce p95 latency for a chat application by selecting an appropriately sized model and adding response caching for repeated prompts. Which pillar best describes this work?

options.explanation: Selecting the right model and using caching to meet latency targets is core Performance Efficiency.
While related, the explicit goal here is meeting performance targets, not environmental impact.
Security is unrelated to latency optimization.
Reliability addresses availability/recovery, not latency tuning.

questions.question: A company is building responsible-AI controls into a Bedrock workload during a Well-Architected review. Which TWO are appropriate Security/responsible-AI best practices?

options.text: Apply Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to block denied topics and filter harmful content
options.explanation: Guardrails enforce content policies, a key responsible-AI safeguard.

options.text: Redact PII from source data before indexing it for retrieval
options.explanation: Removing PII before use limits exposure of sensitive data, a Security best practice.

options.text: Grant the application a wildcard IAM policy for all Bedrock actions
options.explanation: Wildcard permissions violate least privilege.

options.text: Log nothing to reduce storage costs
options.explanation: Disabling audit logging undermines security and compliance.

options.text: Disable encryption to speed up inference
options.explanation: Turning off encryption directly violates the Security pillar.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the relationship between the Well-Architected Framework and a Lens such as the Generative AI Lens?

options.text: A Lens extends the framework with domain-specific design principles and best practices for a particular workload type
options.explanation: Lenses layer specialized guidance (e.g., GenAI, Serverless, ML) on top of the six general pillars.

options.text: A Lens replaces the six pillars entirely for that workload
options.explanation: Lenses augment, not replace, the core pillars.

options.text: A Lens is a billing construct that discounts the workload
options.explanation: Lenses are guidance, not pricing mechanisms.

options.text: A Lens is a runtime guardrail enforced by Bedrock
options.explanation: Lenses are review guidance, not enforced runtime controls.

questions.question: A developer can invoke Anthropic Claude, Meta Llama, Cohere, AI21, Stability AI, and Amazon Nova/Titan models through one consistent interface. What does Amazon Bedrock provide to make this possible?

options.text: A single unified API to access multiple foundation models from different providers
options.explanation: Bedrock exposes one API surface (e.g., InvokeModel/Converse) across many providers' models.

options.text: A separate SDK that must be installed per model provider
options.explanation: Bedrock's value is one API, not per-provider SDKs.

options.text: Direct SSH access to each provider's servers
options.explanation: Bedrock is a fully managed service; you never access provider servers.

options.text: A requirement to self-host each model on Amazon EC2
options.explanation: Bedrock is serverless and managed; self-hosting is not required.

questions.question: A team enabled a foundation model in us-east-1 but gets an access error when calling the same model in eu-west-1. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Model access in Amazon Bedrock is granted per Region and must be enabled in each Region
options.explanation: Bedrock model access is a per-Region setting; enabling it in one Region does not enable it elsewhere.

options.text: Foundation models can only ever run in us-east-1
options.explanation: Models are available in many Regions; access is just granted per Region.

options.text: The account must switch to a different AWS partition
options.explanation: Partitions are unrelated; this is a per-Region access grant.

options.text: Provisioned Throughput is required before any Region works
options.explanation: On-demand access works once granted; the issue is the per-Region grant, not throughput.

questions.question: During peak load a single-Region Bedrock application is throttled ("Too many requests"). The team must raise throughput using the same model and same API with minimal operational overhead. What should they use?

options.text: A cross-Region inference profile to distribute traffic across Regions in the geography
options.explanation: Cross-Region inference profiles automatically route invocations across Regions to absorb spikes, with no code or model change.

options.text: Intelligent Prompt Routing across two different models
options.explanation: Prompt routing uses different models in a family; the requirement is the same model.

options.text: Provisioned Throughput sized for the peak
options.explanation: Provisioned Throughput suits steady high volume and adds cost for occasional peaks.

options.text: A custom Lambda that retries in another Region
options.explanation: A custom failover Lambda adds operational overhead vs the built-in profile.

questions.question: A platform team wants to track and attribute Bedrock inference costs per internal team while using cross-Region inference. Which capability lets them tag usage for cost tracking?

options.text: Application inference profiles, which are user-created and support tagging for cost allocation
options.explanation: Application inference profiles are custom profiles you create and tag, enabling cost tracking; system-defined profiles are AWS-managed.

options.text: System-defined inference profiles, which you create and tag yourself
options.explanation: System-defined profiles are predefined by AWS and are not user-tagged; application profiles are the taggable, custom ones.
That detects spend anomalies; it does not create taggable inference routing profiles.
Model Units reserve capacity; they are not the per-team tagging mechanism for cross-Region inference.

questions.question: An IAM policy must restrict a role so it can only invoke a specific application inference profile. Which condition key is appropriate?

options.explanation: This condition key scopes access to a particular inference profile ARN.
That enforces guardrail usage, not inference-profile scoping.
That scopes access to a prompt router, not an inference profile.
That restricts by VPC endpoint, unrelated to inference profiles.

questions.question: A company needs guaranteed, consistent model capacity for a high-volume production workload and predictable performance. Which Amazon Bedrock option fits best?

options.text: Provisioned Throughput, which reserves capacity measured in Model Units
options.explanation: Provisioned Throughput guarantees dedicated capacity (per Model Unit) for steady, predictable workloads.
On-demand is convenient but does not guarantee reserved capacity at peak.
Prompt routing optimizes cost/quality across a family; it does not reserve capacity.
Profiles spread load but do not reserve guaranteed capacity per model unit.

questions.question: A team wants to deploy and serve a custom model they fine-tuned in Amazon Bedrock. Which prerequisite must they satisfy to run inference on the customized model?

options.text: Purchase Provisioned Throughput for the customized model
options.explanation: Customized (fine-tuned) Bedrock models are served via Provisioned Throughput, which is required to host them.

options.text: Nothing — fine-tuned models are always available on-demand for free
options.explanation: Custom models require Provisioned Throughput to serve, unlike base models on-demand.

options.text: Convert the model to ONNX and host on EC2
options.explanation: Bedrock manages serving; manual EC2 hosting is not the mechanism.
Profiles route base-model traffic; they are not the requirement for serving a custom model.

questions.question: An application receives a mix of trivial and complex prompts. The team wants to send easy prompts to a cheaper model and hard prompts to a stronger model in the same family, through one endpoint, to cut cost ~30% without losing quality. What should they use?

options.text: Amazon Bedrock Intelligent Prompt Routing (a prompt router)
options.explanation: A prompt router analyzes each prompt and routes to the best model within the same family via a single serverless endpoint, reducing cost.

options.text: Provisioned Throughput on the larger model
options.explanation: That reserves capacity but does not route by complexity or save cost on easy prompts.

options.text: Two unrelated models with manual if/else in code
options.explanation: Hand-rolled routing adds maintenance; the managed prompt router is the intended solution.
Profiles spread load across Regions; they do not route by prompt complexity.

questions.question: A configuration restricts which prompt router a role can invoke. Which IAM condition key applies?

options.explanation: This condition key scopes invocation to a specific prompt router ARN.
That scopes inference profiles, not prompt routers.
There is no router-scoping behavior in a plain model-id key for this purpose.
That pins a guardrail version, unrelated to routers.

questions.question: A requirement for Intelligent Prompt Routing must be respected when configuring a router. Which statement is correct?

options.text: The router routes between two or more models from the same model family
options.explanation: Prompt routers operate within a single family (e.g., Anthropic, Llama, or Nova variants).

options.text: The router can route between any models from any providers
options.explanation: Routing is constrained to one model family, not arbitrary cross-provider mixing.

options.text: The router requires Provisioned Throughput on both models
options.explanation: Routing is serverless and does not mandate provisioned capacity.

options.text: The router only works for embeddings models
options.explanation: Prompt routing targets text-generation models, not embeddings.

questions.question: A company has thousands of labeled prompt-and-response examples and wants the model to adapt to their specific task using that supervised data. Which Bedrock customization technique fits?

options.explanation: Fine-tuning trains on labeled input/output pairs to specialize a model for a task.
Continued pre-training uses unlabeled domain text, not labeled task examples.
Distillation transfers knowledge from a teacher to a smaller student; it is not the labeled-data adaptation described.
RAG augments prompts at inference time; it does not train the model.

questions.question: A company has a large corpus of unlabeled internal documents in a specialized domain and wants the base model to absorb domain knowledge and vocabulary. Which technique is most appropriate?

options.text: Continued pre-training on the raw domain text (unsupervised)
options.explanation: Continued pre-training ingests unlabeled domain data to build domain familiarity.
Fine-tuning needs labeled task pairs; here the data is unlabeled.
Prompt engineering does not add domain knowledge to the model weights.
Distillation compresses a model; it does not inject a new unlabeled domain corpus.

questions.question: A team wants a smaller, cheaper, faster model that mimics the behavior of a larger high-quality model on their task. Which approach does Amazon Bedrock offer for this?

options.text: Model distillation — train a smaller "student" model using outputs from a larger "teacher" model
options.explanation: Distillation transfers a teacher model's behavior into a smaller student for lower cost/latency.
That broadens domain knowledge but does not compress a teacher into a student.
That reserves capacity; it does not produce a smaller model.
Hybrid search is a retrieval technique, not a model-compression method.

questions.question: A team wants to customize a foundation model while training only a small number of parameters to reduce cost, and to maintain several lightweight task variants on one base model. Which TWO parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) techniques apply?

options.text: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation), which adds small trainable matrices into model layers
options.explanation: LoRA injects low-rank matrices so only a small set of parameters is trained, cutting cost.

options.text: Adapters, which insert small trainable layers between frozen model layers
options.explanation: Adapter modules add a few trainable layers while the base model stays frozen, enabling multiple variants.

options.text: Full-parameter retraining of the entire base model
options.explanation: Full retraining is the opposite of parameter-efficient; it updates all weights at high cost.
Temperature is an inference setting, not a training technique.
Inference mode is unrelated to PEFT training.

questions.question: What is a key practical benefit of using adapters/LoRA (PEFT) rather than full fine-tuning when serving many task-specific behaviors?

options.text: Several small adapters can be maintained for one base model to provide different "flavors" at lower training cost
options.explanation: PEFT lets you keep one base model and swap small adapters, reducing training cost and storage per variant.

options.text: It guarantees the model will never hallucinate
options.explanation: No training method guarantees zero hallucination.

options.text: It removes the need for any training data
options.explanation: PEFT still requires task data; it just trains fewer parameters.

options.text: It eliminates the need for Provisioned Throughput for custom models
options.explanation: Serving customized models still uses Provisioned Throughput.

questions.question: An MLOps team needs a versioned catalog of trained models with approval status that integrates into their CI/CD release process. Which service provides this?

options.explanation: The Model Registry stores versioned models with approval states and integrates with CI/CD pipelines.
Prompt Management versions prompts, not trained models.
CodeArtifact is a software package repository, not a model registry.
ECR stores container images; the model-versioning/approval catalog is the Model Registry.

questions.question: A team must choose a foundation model and wants to compare candidates on accuracy, robustness, and toxicity using both automatic metrics and human review before committing. Which Bedrock capability supports this?

options.text: Amazon Bedrock model evaluation (automatic and human-based evaluation jobs)
options.explanation: Bedrock model evaluation runs automatic and human evaluations to compare models on quality and responsible-AI metrics.
That reserves capacity; it does not evaluate model quality.
Glue Data Quality checks datasets, not model output quality.
Profiles route traffic; they do not evaluate models.

questions.question: A startup has spiky, unpredictable, low-to-moderate Bedrock usage and wants to pay only for what it uses without reserving capacity. Which inference option is most appropriate?

options.explanation: On-demand bills per request and needs no capacity reservation, ideal for variable low/moderate usage.
Provisioned Throughput reserves (and bills for) capacity, wasteful for spiky low usage.
Self-managed GPUs add cost and operational overhead versus on-demand Bedrock.
Batch transform is for offline bulk jobs, not interactive on-demand calls.

questions.question: A team needs to process millions of documents through a foundation model overnight at the lowest cost, with no latency requirement. Which Amazon Bedrock approach fits best?

options.explanation: Batch inference processes large datasets asynchronously at lower cost than real-time, ideal when latency does not matter.

options.text: Synchronous on-demand InvokeModel per document in a tight loop
options.explanation: Real-time per-call invocation is costlier and slower for huge offline volumes.

options.text: Provisioned Throughput sized for daytime peaks
options.explanation: Reserving real-time capacity is unnecessary and costlier for an offline batch.
Routing optimizes per-prompt model choice; it is not a bulk offline mechanism.

questions.question: A company already trained a custom large language model with open weights outside AWS and wants to serve it through the Amazon Bedrock API alongside managed models. Which feature enables this?

options.explanation: Custom Model Import lets you bring supported open-weight custom models and serve them via Bedrock.
That trains a Bedrock base model further; it does not import external weights.
Prompt Flows orchestrate prompts; they do not import models.
Knowledge Bases provide RAG, not model import.

questions.question: A model selection review weighs latency, cost, context window, modality, and quality for a summarization feature. Which statement reflects sound model-selection practice?

options.text: Choose the smallest/cheapest model that meets the task's quality and latency requirements
options.explanation: Right-sizing to requirements balances cost, latency, and quality rather than defaulting to the biggest model.

options.text: Always pick the model with the largest parameter count
options.explanation: Bigger is not always better; it raises cost/latency without guaranteed task benefit.

options.text: Pick whichever model was released most recently
options.explanation: Recency alone is not a selection criterion; fit to requirements matters.

options.text: Choose based only on provider brand
options.explanation: Brand is not a substitute for evaluating fit, cost, and performance.

questions.question: Which description best fits the Amazon Titan and Amazon Nova model families available in Bedrock?

options.text: Amazon's own first-party foundation models (text, embeddings, and multimodal options) offered through Bedrock
options.explanation: Titan and Nova are Amazon-built FMs accessible via the Bedrock unified API, including multimodal Nova variants.

options.text: Third-party models that require a separate Anthropic account
options.explanation: Titan/Nova are Amazon's own models, not Anthropic's.
They are cloud models served through Bedrock.
They are foundation models, not IAM tooling.

questions.question: Before fine-tuning a base model in Amazon Bedrock, which statement about capacity is correct?

options.text: Serving the resulting fine-tuned model requires Provisioned Throughput
options.explanation: Custom/fine-tuned Bedrock models are hosted via Provisioned Throughput.

options.text: Fine-tuned models are always served on-demand at no extra cost
options.explanation: Custom models need Provisioned Throughput, unlike base models on-demand.
Bedrock supports fine-tuning for supported models.

options.text: Fine-tuning requires migrating to Amazon EC2
options.explanation: Bedrock manages the training; EC2 migration is not required.

questions.question: An architect is designing for resilience of FM calls in a critical workflow. Which TWO are valid resilience patterns?

options.text: Use a Step Functions circuit breaker to stop calling the FM when failures exceed a threshold
options.explanation: Circuit breakers protect the system from a failing dependency.

options.text: Fall back to a cheaper or cached response (graceful degradation) when the FM is unavailable
options.explanation: Graceful degradation preserves a usable experience during failures.

options.text: Remove all timeouts so calls wait indefinitely
options.explanation: Unbounded waits worsen reliability and can exhaust resources.

options.text: Hard-code a single Region with no failover
options.explanation: A single Region with no failover is a single point of failure.
top-p is an inference parameter and has nothing to do with availability.

questions.question: A developer wants the difference between a system-defined inference profile and an application inference profile. Which is correct?

options.text: System-defined profiles are predefined by AWS; application profiles are user-created and support tagging for cost tracking
options.explanation: System-defined = automatic/AWS-managed; application = custom and taggable for cost allocation.

options.text: System-defined profiles are user-created; application profiles are AWS-managed
options.explanation: This reverses the definitions.
They differ in who creates them and whether they support custom tagging.

options.text: Only application profiles can route across Regions
options.explanation: Both can support cross-Region routing; the distinction is creation/tagging.

questions.question: A FinOps team wants per-application cost attribution for Bedrock inference without changing application code that already calls a model directly. Which is the most appropriate first step?

options.text: Route calls through application inference profiles and tag them per application
options.explanation: Application inference profiles add taggable, attributable usage for cost tracking across applications.

options.text: Switch every model to Provisioned Throughput
options.explanation: That reserves capacity and changes cost model but does not provide per-app tagging by itself.
Removing logging reduces visibility, the opposite of what is needed.

options.text: Move to a single shared IAM user
options.explanation: Sharing identities harms attribution and security.

questions.question: Which scenario is the best fit for Provisioned Throughput rather than on-demand inference?

options.text: A consistently high-volume production workload needing predictable, guaranteed capacity
options.explanation: Provisioned Throughput shines for steady, high, predictable demand.

options.text: An internal proof-of-concept with a few requests per day
options.explanation: On-demand is cheaper for sporadic low usage.
Batch inference is better for one-off offline volume.

options.text: Occasional traffic spikes a few hours per week
options.explanation: Cross-Region inference profiles better absorb occasional spikes without reserving capacity.

questions.question: A team wants to swap the Bedrock model ID used by a running application based on an operational decision, with an auditable rollout and quick rollback, and no redeploy. Which combination is best?

options.text: Store the model ID in AWS AppConfig and read it at runtime
options.explanation: AppConfig externalizes the model choice for safe, no-redeploy, rollback-friendly changes.

options.text: Hard-code the model ID and redeploy each time
options.explanation: Redeploying for each change is exactly what AppConfig avoids.

options.text: Embed the model ID in a Lambda layer
options.explanation: Layers still require redeployment to change values.

options.text: Use Provisioned Throughput to change the model
options.explanation: Provisioned Throughput reserves capacity; it is not a config switch.

questions.question: Distillation in Amazon Bedrock is described as training a "student" using a "teacher." What is the primary motivation?

options.text: Produce a smaller, lower-cost, lower-latency model that approximates the teacher's quality on the task
options.explanation: Distillation trades a little quality for big cost/latency savings by compressing the teacher into a student.

options.text: Increase the model's parameter count for higher accuracy
options.explanation: Distillation reduces size, not increases it.

options.text: Add unlabeled domain data to the base model
options.explanation: That describes continued pre-training, not distillation.
No technique guarantees zero hallucination.

questions.question: A team must enforce that a particular role can only call models through one approved cross-Region inference profile. Which approach is correct?

options.text: Attach an IAM policy using the bedrock:InferenceProfileArn condition key scoped to that profile
options.explanation: The condition key restricts invocation to the approved inference profile ARN.

options.text: Rely on the application code to call the right profile
options.explanation: Code-only controls are not enforceable security; use IAM conditions.
That enforces guardrails, not inference-profile scoping.
That is a blunt change unrelated to scoping a specific profile.

questions.question: A data engineering team wants to automatically detect anomalies and measure the quality of data in their data lake/ETL pipelines before it is used for training. Which service is purpose-built for this?

options.explanation: Glue Data Quality computes rules and automatically detects anomalies in datasets within Glue pipelines.
Textract extracts text from documents; it does not measure dataset quality.
Transcribe converts speech to text; unrelated to data-quality checks.
Comprehend performs NLP analysis, not pipeline data-quality scoring.

questions.question: A data scientist wants a visual, GUI-based tool to import, explore, transform, and prepare tabular data for ML with minimal code. Which tool fits best?

options.explanation: Data Wrangler is the low-code GUI for importing, visualizing, and transforming data for ML.
Processing runs code-based processing jobs; it is not the GUI prep tool.
Glue Data Quality scores data quality; it is not a visual data-prep canvas.
Comprehend analyzes text; it is not a tabular data-prep GUI.

questions.question: A team needs to run scalable, code-based data processing jobs (cleaning, feature transforms, batch scoring) on managed infrastructure as part of an ML workflow. Which service is designed for this?

options.explanation: SageMaker Processing runs containerized data-processing/feature-engineering jobs on managed compute.
Data Wrangler is interactive GUI prep; Processing is for programmatic jobs at scale.
Transcribe is speech-to-text, not a processing-job service.
Kendra is enterprise search, not a data-processing job runner.

questions.question: A pipeline must convert recorded customer support phone calls into text so the transcripts can be fed to a foundation model. Which service performs the speech-to-text step?

options.explanation: Transcribe converts audio speech to text, ideal for preparing call transcripts.
Polly is text-to-speech, the reverse operation.
Textract extracts text from documents/images, not audio.
Comprehend analyzes text; it does not transcribe audio.

questions.question: A company must extract text, forms, and tables from scanned PDF invoices to feed downstream processing. Which service is purpose-built for this?

options.explanation: Textract extracts printed/handwritten text, forms, and tables from scanned documents.
Transcribe handles audio, not documents.
Comprehend analyzes already-extracted text; it does not OCR documents.
Rekognition analyzes images/video for objects and faces, not structured document text extraction.

questions.question: Before indexing customer emails for a GenAI search app, a team must detect and redact personally identifiable information (PII) from the text. Which managed NLP service does this?

options.explanation: Comprehend detects and can redact PII entities from text, suited to pre-processing for FMs.
Macie discovers/classifies sensitive data in S3 for security posture; it is not a text PII-redaction step for GenAI pipelines.
Textract extracts text; it does not redact PII.
Transcribe converts audio to text; it is not a PII redactor for documents.

questions.question: Where can Amazon Comprehend add value in a foundation-model pipeline? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Pre-processing input text (e.g., detecting entities/sentiment, redacting PII) before it reaches the FM
options.explanation: Comprehend can clean and enrich inputs before inference.

options.text: Post-processing FM output (e.g., scanning generated text for PII or sentiment)

options.explanation: IAM users use long-lived keys and do not federate the IdP.

options.text: Create an IAM role with STS AssumeRole but store IAM user credentials in app config
options.explanation: Storing IAM user credentials violates the no-long-lived-credentials requirement.

options.text: Use an API Gateway Lambda authorizer validating against LDAP and issuing JWTs
options.explanation: Custom LDAP/JWT does not provide temporary AWS credentials or native IdP federation.

questions.question: A Bedrock RAG app retrieves many relevant docs, but the most relevant info often appears lower in results. Which TWO steps improve relevance ranking with MINIMAL operational overhead? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock reranker models with Amazon OpenSearch Service to reorder results by semantic relevance
options.explanation: Bedrock rerankers are purpose-built to reorder results so the most relevant surface first.

options.text: Use Knowledge Bases with hybrid search and OpenSearch Serverless to combine vectors with keyword matching
options.explanation: Hybrid search improves retrieval relevance by blending semantic and keyword matching.

options.text: Configure OpenSearch Serverless with Learning to Rank for relevance scoring
options.explanation: Learning to Rank requires custom trained/maintained models — more overhead.

options.text: Create Aurora PostgreSQL with pgvector and a custom similarity scoring algorithm
options.explanation: Custom scoring and DB management add operational overhead.

options.text: Use SageMaker JumpStart FMs with Kendra Intelligent Ranking and custom relevancy algorithms
options.explanation: Bedrock built-in reranking is simpler and more integrated than custom ranking.

questions.question: A company must deploy existing Python agent code to Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Runtime with minimal overhead, auto-managing HTTP server, endpoint routing, and health monitoring, supporting sub-second and streaming responses. Which TWO approaches fit? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Use the AgentCore SDK with the @app.entrypoint decorator to auto-handle server setup and endpoints
options.explanation: The SDK auto-creates the server on port 8080, the /invocations and /ping endpoints, and handles JSON/streaming.

options.text: Use the AgentCore starter toolkit for automated packaging, containerization, and deployment
options.explanation: The toolkit automates ARM64 build, ECR push, and CreateAgentRuntime deployment with minimal overhead.

options.text: Implement a FastAPI server with /invocations and /ping and container orchestration
options.explanation: Manual FastAPI/Docker/orchestration adds overhead.

options.text: Deploy on Amazon ECS Fargate using a custom container image running the AgentCore SDK
options.explanation: Custom containers/task definitions add management overhead with no auto server/health setup.

options.text: Deploy on Amazon SageMaker real-time endpoints with a custom inference container
options.explanation: Custom inference containers and autoscaling add infrastructure overhead.

questions.question: A diagnostic imaging app must run similarity searches across 50 million images, processed daily but searched infrequently, with a cost-effective, responsive, no-infrastructure solution. Which is MOST cost-effective?

options.text: Create an Amazon S3 vector bucket with vector indexes to store image embeddings and perform similarity searches
options.explanation: S3 Vectors is serverless, scales to billions of vectors, and is cost-optimized for infrequent searches.

options.text: Store vectors in Amazon OpenSearch Serverless and use vector search
options.explanation: OpenSearch Serverless suits frequent low-latency search; provisioned capacity is underused for rare searches.

options.text: Use DynamoDB to store vectors and custom similarity logic in Lambda
options.explanation: DynamoDB has no native vector search; custom logic adds latency and cost.

options.text: Store vectors in RDS for PostgreSQL with pgvector
options.explanation: RDS requires managing instances and is not cost-effective for infrequent workloads.

questions.question: A single-Region Bedrock app gets "Too many requests" at peak. The company must increase throughput without extra operational overhead, keep the same API, and use the same FM. Which is MOST cost-effective?

options.text: Use cross-Region inference to distribute traffic across multiple Regions in the geography
options.explanation: Cross-Region inference automatically spreads load across Regions, same model and API, no extra ops.

options.text: Use prompt routing to distribute traffic across multiple FMs from the same family
options.explanation: Prompt routing needs two different models; the requirement is the same FM.

options.text: Use provisioned throughput to provision higher throughput
options.explanation: Provisioned throughput suits steady usage, not occasional peaks.

options.text: Create a Lambda that defaults to the original Region and falls back to a secondary Region
options.explanation: A custom Lambda failover adds operational overhead.

questions.question: An AI assistant must include new S3 documents ASAP and exclude deleted ones ASAP, using a Bedrock Knowledge Base with S3 as the data source. Which solution is scalable, event-driven, and resilient?

options.text: S3 Event Notifications → Amazon SQS → Lambda calling IngestKnowledgeBaseDocuments / DeleteKnowledgeBaseDocuments
options.explanation: SQS buffers spikes and retries between S3 events and Lambda, ensuring no updates are missed.

options.text: S3 Event Notifications → Lambda directly calling the ingest/delete APIs
options.explanation: Direct invocation lacks buffering/retry and can fail under load.

options.text: EventBridge Scheduler every 5 minutes → Lambda that tracks S3 changes
options.explanation: Time-based polling is not near-real-time and is inefficient.

options.text: EventBridge Scheduler every 5 minutes → Lambda that runs StartIngestionJob
options.explanation: Periodic full re-sync is inefficient and not event-driven.

questions.question: A RAG app returns generic "no relevant information found" after a code update. No errors, FM invokes successfully (X-Ray), OpenSearch is healthy, latency normal. What is the cause?

options.text: The updated Lambda uses a different version of the embedding model than was used to index documents
options.explanation: Embedding drift creates a vector-space mismatch between queries and stored vectors, silently breaking retrieval.

options.text: The embeddings were deleted during the update and not re-indexed
options.explanation: Deleted embeddings would surface as errors or failed queries; none are observed.

options.text: The Lambda role is missing bedrock:InvokeModel
options.explanation: The model invokes successfully, so the permission exists.

options.text: The FM temperature parameter was increased
options.explanation: Temperature affects generation, not retrieval.

questions.question: A Bedrock AI assistant uses multiple guardrails (prompt injection, sensitive info filtering, denied topics). When content is blocked, the developer needs the MOST detailed analysis of which specific guardrail rule was invoked. Which configuration provides it?

options.text: Enable guardrail tracing ({"trace":"enabled"}) and monitor InvocationsIntervened filtered by GuardrailPolicyType (ContentPolicy, TopicPolicy, SensitiveInformationPolicy)
options.explanation: GuardrailPolicyType reveals which policy intervened, the most detailed breakdown.

options.text: Enable guardrail tracing and filter InvocationsIntervened by GuardrailContentSource
options.explanation: GuardrailContentSource only distinguishes input vs output, not which policy fired.

options.text: Enable model invocation logging with CloudWatch alarms on InvocationsIntervened by GuardrailContentSource
options.explanation: Invocation logging lacks per-policy intervention detail.

options.text: Enable Bedrock model evaluation jobs with guardrail assessment metrics
options.explanation: Model evaluation runs offline tests, not per-inference guardrail tracing.

questions.question: A financial services company wants a mobile app for account inquiries built from large volumes of customer-support email stored in S3 that contains PII which must not appear in search results. Which solution meets these requirements?

options.text: Use Amazon Comprehend to detect and redact PII from the email data, then integrate with Amazon Kendra for enterprise search
options.explanation: Comprehend redacts PII before indexing; Kendra provides managed enterprise search of the processed data.

options.text: Use Amazon Kendra and a Bedrock FM system prompt to remove PII during query processing
options.explanation: System prompts cannot reliably remove PII and can be bypassed.

options.text: Use Amazon Textract to extract text, Amazon Macie to scan for PII, integrate with Kendra
options.explanation: Macie discovers/classifies PII but does not redact it for GenAI; Textract targets scanned docs, not raw email.

options.text: Use Amazon Comprehend to redact PII and integrate with Amazon DocumentDB for queries
options.explanation: DocumentDB needs custom search and lacks NLP for natural-language enterprise search.

questions.question: A company must analyze videos and photos from public fashion shows to extract style elements, store results, and present a trend dashboard, with the LEAST operational overhead. Which solution fits?

options.text: Use Step Functions to process media with Amazon Bedrock multimodal FMs; store results in S3; visualize with Amazon QuickSight
options.explanation: Bedrock multimodal FMs analyze media directly with no custom model training; Step Functions + QuickSight are serverless.

options.text: Use EventBridge + Lambda with Amazon QuickSight Q to analyze videos/photos and a QuickSight dashboard
options.explanation: QuickSight Q answers questions about existing dashboard data; it cannot analyze media.

options.text: Use Rekognition Custom Labels (trained model), store in DynamoDB, build a Managed Grafana dashboard with custom plugins
options.explanation: Custom model training and Grafana plugins add overhead.

options.text: Use Claude for text analysis and Stable Diffusion for images, store in OpenSearch, build Managed Grafana
options.explanation: Managing OpenSearch, multiple FMs, and custom dashboards adds overhead, and it analyzes text rather than visuals.

questions.question: A financial company needs Bedrock prompt templates governed via approval workflows and comprehensive logging of all model invocations with 7-year retention. Which TWO steps meet this with MINIMAL overhead? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management with multi-stage approval workflows and IAM multi-party authorization
options.explanation: Prompt Management natively handles versioned templates with multi-stage approvals.

options.text: Enable Bedrock model invocation logging to S3 with S3 Object Lock (compliance mode) for 7 years, with per-business-unit prefixes
options.explanation: Invocation logging plus immutable Object Lock storage meets comprehensive 7-year retention with low overhead.

options.text: Store prompt templates in DynamoDB with item-level permissions for approvals
options.explanation: Custom approval logic in DynamoDB adds development overhead.

options.text: Use EventBridge to capture invocation events to CloudWatch Logs then export to S3 Object Lock
options.explanation: Custom pipelines add failure points and do not provide a complete audit trail.

options.text: Enable CloudTrail data events for all Bedrock APIs into CloudTrail Lake with 7-year retention
options.explanation: CloudTrail captures API metadata, not full prompt/response content.

questions.question: A company requires that all InvokeModel and Converse calls apply Bedrock guardrails, enforced in the MOST operationally efficient way. Which solution fits?

options.text: Configure IAM policies for InvokeModel/Converse with the bedrock:GuardrailIdentifier condition key on all roles that access Bedrock FMs
options.explanation: The GuardrailIdentifier condition key centrally enforces guardrail usage with no custom code.

options.text: Create a Lambda that validates guardrails before proxying all requests to Bedrock
options.explanation: A proxy Lambda adds a bottleneck/failure point and custom maintenance.

options.text: Store guardrail IDs in SSM Parameter Store and have a Lambda fetch them per call
options.explanation: Per-call lookups add latency and Lambda maintenance.

options.text: Configure IAM policies with both GuardrailIdentifier and PromptRouterArn condition keys
options.explanation: PromptRouterArn is unrelated to guardrail enforcement and adds needless complexity.

questions.question: A virtual assistant using Anthropic Claude on Bedrock must stop generating output after a specific phrase appears in the response. Which solution meets this?

options.text: Use the stop sequences parameter in the inference call to specify the trigger phrase
options.explanation: Stop sequences are a built-in, reliable API mechanism that halts generation at the specified string.

options.text: Add the trigger phrase "stop at this phrase" in the user prompt
options.explanation: Relying on the model to follow the instruction is unreliable.

options.text: Use the top-k parameter to control token diversity
options.explanation: top-k controls sampling, not stopping.

options.text: Use the temperature parameter to control the phrase likelihood
options.explanation: Temperature controls randomness, not the stopping point.

questions.question: A cross-functional team wants to use Amazon Q Developer to optimize productivity, enforce integration patterns, automate performance tuning, and accelerate testing. Which TWO steps meet these requirements? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Configure Q Developer to auto-generate/refactor integration code, give targeted API guidance, and suggest performance optimizations across the codebase
options.explanation: Real-time generation, refactoring, and optimization boost productivity and reduce integration errors.

options.text: Integrate Q Developer automated unit/integration test generation into the CI/CD pipelines
options.explanation: Embedding test generation in CI/CD continuously validates components and catches defects early.

options.text: Use Q Developer for security analysis but require manual security-team approval of all changes
options.explanation: Mandatory manual approval of everything creates a bottleneck.

options.text: Use Q Developer to retrospectively document integration patterns across codebases
options.explanation: Reactive analysis misses real-time assistance benefits.

options.text: Limit Q Developer to merge-request fixes with periodic manual refactoring cycles
options.explanation: Restricting to merge time slows resolution and underuses its capabilities.

questions.question: A DJL-served LLM on 8-GPU instances is underutilized: real max sequence length is 10x smaller than configured, concurrency is low, and the model fits within 4 GPUs. Which TWO steps improve resource utilization? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Reduce the model's maximum sequence length to allow a higher rolling batch size per GPU
options.explanation: Freeing memory by lowering max sequence length increases batch size, throughput, and concurrency.

options.text: Use tensor parallelism with a degree of 4 to deploy two model replicas per instance
options.explanation: Since the model fits in 4 GPUs, degree 4 packs two replicas across 8 GPUs for full utilization.

options.text: Increase the number of instances and spread requests more evenly
options.explanation: This does not raise per-instance concurrency or reduce the memory footprint.

options.text: Enable speculative decoding to reduce per-request latency
options.explanation: Speculative decoding improves latency, not utilization.

options.text: Use tensor parallelism degree 8 to split the model across all GPUs
options.explanation: Degree 8 serves only one replica across 8 GPUs, leaving half idle since the model fits in 4.

questions.question: A developer must deploy a Hugging Face text-to-image model (tested in SageMaker JumpStart) for on-demand generation. It must use GPUs, handle text datasets up to 50 MB, and respond within 15 minutes. Which deployment strategy fits?

options.text: Deploy a SageMaker Asynchronous Inference endpoint on an accelerated (GPU) instance, invoked on demand via Lambda
options.explanation: Async endpoints support up to ~15-minute runtimes, large payloads (up to ~1 GB), and GPUs.

options.text: Deploy a SageMaker Serverless Inference endpoint on a general-purpose instance
options.explanation: Serverless inference does not support GPUs.

options.text: Deploy a SageMaker Real-Time endpoint on a GPU instance
options.explanation: Real-time endpoints cap payloads near 25 MB, failing the 50 MB requirement.

options.text: Create a SageMaker batch transform job on a GPU instance
options.explanation: Batch transform is offline, not on-demand within 15 minutes.

questions.question: A news company wants a web editor where journalists click "analyze" and immediately receive streamed style-guide revisions; articles carry content-category metadata. Which architecture meets this with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.text: API Gateway WebSocket API → Lambda reads the category metadata, routes to the right Bedrock model, uses Prompt Management for style rules, and streams suggestions via the Bedrock streaming API
options.explanation: WebSockets give serverless real-time streaming; Prompt Management governs rules; streaming returns suggestions immediately.

options.text: SQS ingestion → Step Functions → Lambda → DynamoDB → API Gateway WebSocket streaming
options.explanation: SQS polling adds latency, defeating immediate feedback on click.

options.text: API Gateway REST API with Lambda function URLs and chunked transfer encoding
options.explanation: REST is poorly suited to long-lived real-time streaming.

options.text: Application Load Balancer with ECS tasks running custom routing/style containers
options.explanation: Custom ECS containers add infrastructure management overhead.

questions.question: A Bedrock Knowledge Base ingests documents from multiple S3 buckets, and the team needs to monitor ingestion to identify and troubleshoot document-processing issues. Which solution meets this?

options.text: Configure knowledge base logging to Amazon CloudWatch Logs and use Logs Insights to query for failed document processing
options.explanation: KB logging records per-file status (e.g., RESOURCE_IGNORED, EMBEDDING_FAILED, INDEXING_FAILED) for troubleshooting.

options.text: Enable CloudWatch Application Signals to detect knowledge base performance issues
options.explanation: Application Signals monitors app performance, not KB ingestion.

options.text: Enable AWS CloudTrail to track API calls related to ingestion
options.explanation: CloudTrail logs API calls, not document-level processing status.

options.text: Implement Bedrock model invocation logging to capture document processing metrics
options.explanation: Invocation logging captures inference calls, not ingestion.

questions.question: A customer-support solution needs to route each user query to one of several specialized agents (billing, technical, account) and keep a coherent conversation across them. Which AWS open-source framework is purpose-built for multi-agent orchestration and routing?

options.explanation: Agent Squad orchestrates multiple specialized agents, routing each query to the best-suited agent while maintaining unified conversation context.
Guardrails enforce content safety; they do not orchestrate multiple agents.
Knowledge Bases provide RAG retrieval, not multi-agent routing.
Comprehend is NLP analysis, not an agent orchestration framework.

questions.question: In AWS Agent Squad, how is each incoming user request assigned to the right specialized agent?

options.text: An LLM-powered classifier analyzes the input, agent descriptions, and conversation history to select the best-suited agent
options.explanation: Agent Squad uses a classifier (e.g., the default Bedrock Classifier) to route requests by intent.

options.text: Requests are assigned round-robin regardless of content
options.explanation: Routing is intent-based via the classifier, not blind round-robin.

options.text: The user must manually pick an agent each turn
options.explanation: Routing is automatic; users do not hand-pick agents per turn.

options.text: Agents are chosen by alphabetical order of their names
options.explanation: Selection is based on classification of intent/context, not naming order.

questions.question: Which TWO are accurate about AWS Agent Squad?

options.text: Member agents can be different types, such as Amazon Bedrock model agents, Amazon Lex bots, or AWS Lambda functions
options.explanation: Agent Squad supports heterogeneous agents including Bedrock, Lex, and Lambda-backed agents.

options.text: It maintains unified conversation context across agents for coherent multi-turn interactions
options.explanation: The orchestrator keeps shared context so conversations stay coherent as routing changes.

options.text: It can only run a single agent at a time and has no routing
options.explanation: Its entire purpose is routing across multiple agents.
Agent Squad is an orchestration framework, not a vector store.

options.text: It requires Provisioned Throughput to classify requests
options.explanation: Classification uses a model/classifier; it does not mandate provisioned capacity.

questions.question: In Agent Squad, what is the difference between the classifier's view and an individual agent's view of conversation history?

options.text: The classifier has a global view across agents, while each agent sees only its own conversation history
options.explanation: This lets the classifier route with full context while keeping each agent focused on its domain.

options.text: Every agent always sees the full global history
options.explanation: Agents see only their own history; the global view belongs to the classifier.

options.text: The classifier sees nothing and routes blindly
options.explanation: The classifier specifically uses global context to route.
Agent Squad maintains conversation context.

questions.question: How do AWS Strands Agents and AWS Agent Squad differ in purpose?

options.text: Strands is a model-driven SDK for building an individual agent; Agent Squad orchestrates and routes across multiple specialized agents
options.explanation: Strands builds single agents (model + prompt + tools); Agent Squad coordinates many agents.
They serve different roles: single-agent SDK vs multi-agent orchestration.

options.text: Strands routes between many agents and Agent Squad builds one agent
options.explanation: This reverses their roles.
Neither is a vector store.

questions.question: A team wants an agent to interleave reasoning with tool actions (think, act, observe, repeat) using structured steps orchestrated on AWS. Which pattern/approach matches?

options.text: The ReAct pattern, which can be implemented with AWS Step Functions to structure reasoning and tool-use steps
options.explanation: ReAct (Reason + Act) interleaves reasoning and actions; Step Functions can structure these steps.

options.text: A single one-shot prompt with no tools
options.explanation: One-shot prompting does not interleave reasoning and tool actions.
That reserves capacity; it is not a reasoning pattern.
Sharding is storage tuning, unrelated to agent reasoning.

questions.question: A complex task benefits from breaking the problem into intermediate reasoning steps before producing a final answer. Which prompting technique is this?

options.explanation: Chain-of-thought prompts the model to reason through intermediate steps, improving complex task performance.
Stop sequences end generation; they do not elicit step-by-step reasoning.
top-k controls candidate tokens, not reasoning structure.
Hybrid search is a retrieval technique, not a reasoning method.

questions.question: An agentic workflow must pause for a human to review and approve high-impact actions before continuing. Which approach implements this human-in-the-loop control on AWS?

options.text: Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate a review/approval step (e.g., with a callback) before the agent proceeds
options.explanation: Step Functions can pause for human approval (task tokens/callbacks) to gate sensitive actions.

options.text: Increase the model temperature so it self-approves
options.explanation: Temperature affects randomness, not human approval.

options.text: Remove all guardrails to speed approvals
options.explanation: Removing safeguards is the opposite of controlled human review.

options.text: Store the action in S3 and hope a human notices
options.explanation: Passive storage is not an orchestrated approval mechanism.

questions.question: A developer calls Amazon Bedrock with the InvokeModel API across several providers and finds each model expects a different request body. What is the most accurate statement?

options.text: InvokeModel request/response bodies are model-specific, so the JSON payload must match each model's schema
options.explanation: With InvokeModel you format the body to the chosen model's native request schema.

options.text: InvokeModel uses one identical body for every model
options.explanation: Native InvokeModel bodies differ per provider/model.
Bedrock requests are JSON, and the schema is model-specific.

options.text: InvokeModel cannot be used for text models
options.explanation: InvokeModel is a primary API for text models.

questions.question: A team wants a single, consistent request/response format for multi-turn chat that works across multiple Bedrock models without hand-coding each model's native schema. Which API should they use?

options.text: The Amazon Bedrock Converse API, which provides a unified messages interface across supported models
options.explanation: Converse abstracts model-specific formats into a consistent messages structure with roles, simplifying portability.

options.text: InvokeModel with a model-specific body for each model
options.explanation: InvokeModel requires per-model formatting, which is what they want to avoid.
That ingests data into a Knowledge Base; it is not a chat inference API.
Rerank reorders retrieved documents; it is not a chat interface.

questions.question: When formatting input for a dialog-based application using the Bedrock Converse API, how is the conversation typically structured?

options.text: As a sequence of messages with roles (e.g., user and assistant), plus an optional system instruction
options.explanation: Conversation formatting uses role-tagged messages (user/assistant) and an optional system prompt.

options.text: As a single concatenated string with no roles
options.explanation: Dialog formatting uses structured role-tagged turns, not one undifferentiated string.
Conversations are structured messages, not token CSVs.
Embeddings are for retrieval, not the chat message structure.

questions.question: A pipeline sends requests to an Amazon SageMaker AI real-time endpoint. What determines the required input data format?

options.text: The endpoint/container's expected content type and serializer (e.g., application/json or text/csv) per the model's contract
options.explanation: SageMaker endpoints expect input matching the container's content type and the model's input contract.

options.text: All SageMaker endpoints require the Bedrock Converse schema
options.explanation: SageMaker input depends on the container, not the Bedrock Converse format.

options.text: SageMaker endpoints accept any format without serialization
options.explanation: Input must match the endpoint's expected content type/serializer.
Format depends on the model; it is not audio-only.

questions.question: Inconsistent, messy text is degrading FM response quality. A team wants to normalize and reformat inputs before inference with minimal custom infrastructure. Which approach fits?

options.text: Use AWS Lambda functions to normalize/reformat the data (and optionally Amazon Comprehend to extract entities) before calling the FM
options.explanation: Lambda is a low-overhead way to clean and reformat inputs; Comprehend can enrich them prior to inference.

options.text: Send raw messy text directly and raise temperature
options.explanation: Higher temperature adds randomness and does not fix input quality.

options.text: Provision a large GPU cluster to compensate
options.explanation: Hardware does not fix input formatting/quality issues.

options.text: Disable all preprocessing to save cost
options.explanation: Skipping preprocessing worsens response quality.

questions.question: Why is correct input formatting important when integrating foundation models?

options.text: Models have model-specific input requirements; malformed or mismatched formatting causes errors or poor responses
options.explanation: Matching each model's expected request/message format is necessary for valid calls and good output quality.

options.text: Formatting only affects billing, not behavior
options.explanation: Formatting affects whether the call succeeds and the quality of the response.

options.text: All models accept identical raw input, so formatting never matters
options.explanation: Input requirements vary by model/API; formatting matters.

options.text: Formatting is only relevant for image models
options.explanation: It applies to text, chat, and other modalities, not just images.
